Walmart Data¶

📊 Residual Behavior and the Bell Curve¶

The distribution of a model's residuals (prediction errors) acts as a direct health check for whether the model has successfully learned the data.

1. Non-Normal Residuals (Missing Patterns)¶

  • What it means: If the residuals do not follow a normal distribution, it means a key pattern or relationship has been missed during prediction.
  • The Cause: A predictable, structural trend has been left behind in the errors. The model is making systematic errors (such as consistently underpredicting massive holiday sales surges).

2. Normally Distributed Residuals (Captured Relationships)¶

  • What it means: If the residuals form a clean, symmetrical bell curve, the major underlying patterns and relationships are being handled fine by the model.
  • The Cause: The model has successfully captured the predictable core of the data. What remains are just errors coming from random noise—the millions of tiny, chaotic, and completely independent quirks of daily human behavior.

☕ Real-World Example: The Local Coffee Shop¶

To truly understand pure random noise versus a systematic pattern, imagine you own a small coffee shop. On a normal, average Tuesday, your baseline business is exactly 100 cups of coffee.

You build a simple model that predicts you will sell 100 cups every Tuesday. Let's look at what actually happens over a few weeks:

  • Week 1 (Actual Sales: 102 cups): A regular customer decided to buy an extra coffee for a coworker on a whim.
    • Residual: $102 - 100 = \mathbf{+2}$
  • Week 2 (Actual Sales: 97 cups): A family who usually walks by ran late for school and skipped stopping in.
    • Residual: $97 - 100 = \mathbf{-3}$
  • Week 3 (Actual Sales: 101 cups): It was a little breezier than usual, prompting one extra person to grab a hot drink.
    • Residual: $101 - 100 = \mathbf{+1}$
  • Week 4 (Actual Sales: 96 cups): Two people decided they were drinking too much caffeine and switched to tea at home.
    • Residual: $96 - 100 = \mathbf{-4}$

1. Why this is "Pure Random Noise"¶

Look closely at those errors ($+2, -3, +1, -4$). This is textbook random noise because:

  1. They are completely independent: The family running late for school (Week 2) has absolutely nothing to do with the customer buying their coworker a coffee (Week 1). They are isolated, tiny real-world events.
  2. They are symmetric around zero: The errors shuffle unpredictably between slightly positive and slightly negative. The model isn't consistently guessing too high or too low. On average, they balance out to 0.
  3. They form a Bell Curve: If you tracked this for 500 normal Tuesdays, missing by 1 or 2 cups happens constantly (the tall center of the bell curve), while randomly missing by 50 cups due to random quirks never happens. This is what a healthy model leaves behind.

2. Contrast with a "Systematic Pattern" (The Walmart Problem)¶

Now imagine on Week 5, a massive construction crew starts tearing up the street right outside your shop to fix a water main. You only sell 40 cups of coffee.

  • Residual: $40 - 100 = \mathbf{-60}$

This $-60$ error is not random noise. It is a massive, structural event. If you don't give your model a specific rule to handle construction, this huge error will completely wreck your perfect bell curve.

⚠️ In your dataset, the holiday weeks are the "construction crews." They are massive, highly structured business events, which is why your linear regression errors are jumping completely off the bell curve!

🧠 Why Random Noise Forms a Bell Curve¶

Why should random noise look like a normal distribution?

According to the Central Limit Theorem, when you sum up a vast number of independent, random real-world occurrences, their collective sum will always naturally merge into a Normal Distribution.

Therefore: $$\text{Perfect Model} = \text{Captured Relationships (Signal)} + \text{Sum of Independent Random Quirks (Normal Noise)}$$

🔗 Connection: Error Bell Curve, Loss Minimization & Maximum Likelihood ¶

The Big Idea¶

When we train an ML model, we want the residuals (errors) to follow a bell curve (Normal Distribution). But why? Because this connects three powerful ideas into one unified framework:

Minimizing a Loss Function = Maximizing the Likelihood of your errors under a bell curve

These are not two separate things — they are the same mathematical operation, just written in different languages.

How It Works¶

When we assume errors follow a Normal Distribution, we are saying:

$$\text{error} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu=0, \sigma^2)$$

The likelihood of observing a set of residuals given our model is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Taking the log-likelihood (to simplify the product into a sum):

$$\log \mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) \;-\; \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

To maximize the log-likelihood, we need to minimize:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \quad \longrightarrow \quad \textbf{This is exactly MSE (Mean Squared Error)!}$$

The Decoded Truth: Loss Function = Log-Likelihood in Disguise¶

Every loss function you use in ML is secretly an assumption about the distribution of your errors:

Loss Function Error Distribution Assumed Probabilistic Equivalent
MSE / L2 Loss Normal (Gaussian) MLE on Gaussian
MAE / L1 Loss Laplace MLE on Laplace
Binary Cross-Entropy Bernoulli MLE on Bernoulli
Categorical Cross-Entropy Categorical MLE on Multinomial

What the Optimizer is Actually Doing¶

When you pick MSE as your loss function, you are implicitly making a declaration:

"I believe my errors are Normally distributed around zero."

The optimizer then adjusts the model weights to find the mean (μ) of that bell curve — which is exactly your model's prediction (ŷ). It is finding the parameter that makes the residuals you actually observed the most probable outcome under a Gaussian distribution.

$$\underbrace{\text{Minimize MSE}}_{\text{Loss Minimization}} \;\equiv\; \underbrace{\text{Maximize } \mathcal{L}(\theta)}_{\text{Maximum Likelihood Estimation}}$$

The σ² (variance/spread) of the bell curve does not affect which model weights are optimal — which is why we drop it during training. However, σ² matters when you want confidence intervals and uncertainty estimates around your predictions.

One-Line Summary¶

$$\boxed{\text{Training an ML model with MSE} = \text{Finding the bell curve's mean that best explains your residuals via MLE}}$$

This is the bridge between classical statistics and modern machine learning.

📊 STD of Residuals — Bell Curve Analysis ¶

When residuals (errors) are plotted and form a bell curve (normal distribution), the standard deviation (σ) of that curve is a powerful diagnostic tool.

🔍 What it Measures¶

The typical spread of your model errors — how far off predictions usually are from actual values.

$$\text{Residual} = y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{predicted}}$$

📐 The 68-95-99.7 Rule (Empirical Rule)¶

Range % of Predictions within this Error
± 1σ ~68% of predictions
± 2σ ~95% of predictions
± 3σ ~99.7% of predictions

Example: If σ = $500, then 95% of predictions are within **±$1000** of actual sales.

✅ What to Look For¶

Observation Meaning
Bell curve centered at 0 ✅ Model is unbiased
Bell curve shifted left/right ❌ Model has systematic bias
Narrow bell curve (small σ) ✅ Model is precise & reliable
Wide bell curve (large σ) ❌ Model has high, inconsistent error
Skewed / non-normal shape ❌ Model is missing some pattern

🚀 Where STD of Residuals Helps in Real World¶

1. 🏪 Business Decision Making (Inventory Planning)¶

Walmart needs to decide how much stock to order for next week.

Model predicts: Store 1 will sell $50,000 next week

With σ = $500, Walmart now knows:

"There's a 95% chance actual sales will be between $49,000 and $51,000"

So they order stock accordingly — not too much (wastage), not too little (stockout).

Without this confidence range, they'd just blindly trust $50,000 and risk being wrong.

2. 🔁 Model Comparison¶

You train two models:

Model Predicted Sales Residual STD
Model A $50,000 σ = $500
Model B $50,000 σ = $2,000

Both predict the same value, but Model A is 4x more reliable.

The STD helps you choose the better model even when RMSE alone might not tell the full story.

4. 🚨 Anomaly / Fraud Detection¶

If a store's actual sales fall outside ±3σ, it's statistically unusual (only 0.3% chance naturally). This flags it as:

  • A data entry error
  • An unusual event (theft, store closure)
  • Fraud

The STD acts as a threshold for alerting.

5. 🗣️ Communicating Model Reliability to Non-Technical People¶

Instead of saying "our RMSE is 1.04" (meaningless to business people), you say:

"Our model predicts weekly sales with 95% accuracy within ±$1,000"

That is clear, actionable, and trustworthy to managers and stakeholders.

💡 One Line Summary¶

The STD of the residual bell curve tells you how confident you can be in your model's predictions and within what range the true value is likely to fall.## 📊 STD of Residuals — Bell Curve Analysis

When residuals (errors) are plotted and form a bell curve (normal distribution), the standard deviation (σ) of that curve is a powerful diagnostic tool.

🔍 What it Measures¶

The typical spread of your model errors — how far off predictions usually are from actual values.

$$\text{Residual} = y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{predicted}}$$

Imports¶

ub = mean_sales + std_sales

import scipy.stats as stats
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

stats.probplot(df['Weekly_Sales'], dist="norm", plot=plt)

# Customize labels and title for readability
plt.title('QQ Plot for Weekly Sales', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Theoretical Quantiles (Normal Distribution)', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Ordered Values (Actual Weekly Sales in $)', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

# Display the plot
plt.show()
In [16]:
import scipy.stats as stats
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

X = df[['CPI', 'Holiday_Flag', 'Temperature', 'Unemployment', 'Fuel_Price']] 
y = df['Weekly_Sales']

scaler_X = StandardScaler()
scaler_y = StandardScaler()

X_scaled = scaler_X.fit_transform(X)
y_scaled = scaler_y.fit_transform(y.values.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()

model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X_scaled, y_scaled)
Out[16]:
LinearRegression()
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
fit_intercept fit_intercept: bool, default=True

Whether to calculate the intercept for this model. If set
to False, no intercept will be used in calculations
(i.e. data is expected to be centered). 		True
copy_X copy_X: bool, default=True

If True, X will be copied; else, it may be overwritten. 		True
tol tol: float, default=1e-6

The precision of the solution (`coef_`) is determined by `tol` which
specifies the convergence criterion of the underlying solver. `tol` is
set as `atol` and `btol` of :func:`scipy.sparse.linalg.lsqr` when
fitting on sparse training data. `tol` is set as `cond` of
:func:`scipy.linalg.lstsq` when fitting on dense training data.

.. versionadded:: 1.7
.. versionchanged:: 1.9
Now supported on dense data, interpreted as the `cond` parameter. 		1e-06
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to use for the computation. This will only provide
speedup in case of sufficiently large problems, that is if firstly
`n_targets > 1` and secondly `X` is sparse or if `positive` is set
to `True`. ``None`` means 1 unless in a
:obj:`joblib.parallel_backend` context. ``-1`` means using all
processors. See :term:`Glossary <n_jobs>` for more details. 		None
positive positive: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, forces the coefficients to be positive. This
option is only supported for dense arrays.

For a comparison between a linear regression model with positive constraints
on the regression coefficients and a linear regression without such constraints,
see :ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_linear_model_plot_nnls.py`.

.. versionadded:: 0.24 		False
Fitted attributes
Name Type Value
coef_ coef_: array of shape (n_features, ) or (n_targets, n_features)

Estimated coefficients for the linear regression problem.
If multiple targets are passed during the fit (y 2D), this
is a 2D array of shape (n_targets, n_features), while if only
one target is passed, this is a 1D array of length n_features. 		ndarray[float64](5,) [-0.11, 0.03,-0.02,-0.14,-0.01]

Independent term in the linear model. Set to 0.0 if
`fit_intercept = False`. 		float64 -1.353e-16

Number of features seen during :term:`fit`.

Rank of matrix `X`. Only available when `X` is dense. 		int 5

Singular values of `X`. Only available when `X` is dense. 		ndarray[float64](5,) [92.67,90.03,80.23,75.83,57.4 ]
In [17]:
y_pred_scaled = model.predict(X_scaled)
residuals = y_scaled - y_pred_scaled

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
stats.probplot(residuals, dist="norm", plot=plt)

plt.title('QQ Plot of Residuals (Errors) - Scaled Model', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Theoretical Quantiles', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Ordered Residuals', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.show()
In [18]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scipy.stats as stats

# Set the plot size
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

# 1. Plot the histogram of your actual residuals
# 'density=True' converts counts to probabilities so the bell curve line fits perfectly
count, bins, ignored = plt.hist(residuals, bins=40, density=True, alpha=0.6, 
                                color='skyblue', edgecolor='black', label='Model Residuals (Mistakes)')

# 2. Calculate the perfect theoretical bell curve based on your residuals
mu, sigma = residuals.mean(), residuals.std()
perfect_bell_curve = stats.norm.pdf(bins, mu, sigma)

# 3. Plot the perfect bell curve line
plt.plot(bins, perfect_bell_curve, color='crimson', linewidth=2.5, 
         label=f'Perfect Bell Curve\n(Mean={mu:.2f}, Std={sigma:.2f})')

# 4. Add a vertical line at exactly 0 (where predictions would be perfect)
plt.axvline(0, color='black', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7, label='Zero Error (Perfect Guess)')

# 5. Customize titles and labels
plt.title('Are the Errors Random? (Residuals vs. Perfect Bell Curve)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Size of Prediction Error', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Density', fontsize=12)
plt.legend(fontsize=11)
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

plt.show()
In [19]:
from sklearn.model_selection import learning_curve
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# -------------------------------------------------------
# Learning Curve: Diagnose Overfitting / Underfitting
# -------------------------------------------------------
# Uses the same model and scaled data from cell [9]
# X_scaled = CPI (standardized), y_scaled = Weekly_Sales (standardized)

train_sizes, train_scores, val_scores = learning_curve(
    estimator=LinearRegression(),
    X=X_scaled,
    y=y_scaled,
    train_sizes=np.linspace(0.1, 1.0, 10),
    cv=5,
    scoring='neg_mean_squared_error',
    n_jobs=-1
)

# Convert negative MSE to positive RMSE for readability
train_rmse = np.sqrt(-train_scores.mean(axis=1))
val_rmse   = np.sqrt(-val_scores.mean(axis=1))
train_std  = np.sqrt(-train_scores).std(axis=1)
val_std    = np.sqrt(-val_scores).std(axis=1)

# -------------------------------------------------------
# Plot
# -------------------------------------------------------
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.plot(train_sizes, train_rmse, 'o-', color='steelblue', linewidth=2, label='Training Error (RMSE)')
plt.fill_between(train_sizes, train_rmse - train_std, train_rmse + train_std, alpha=0.15, color='steelblue')

plt.plot(train_sizes, val_rmse, 'o-', color='crimson', linewidth=2, label='Validation Error (RMSE)')
plt.fill_between(train_sizes, val_rmse - val_std, val_rmse + val_std, alpha=0.15, color='crimson')

plt.title('Learning Curve - Linear Regression (CPI vs Weekly Sales)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Training Set Size', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('RMSE (Standardized Scale)', fontsize=12)
plt.legend(fontsize=11)
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# -------------------------------------------------------
# Diagnosis
# -------------------------------------------------------
gap = abs(val_rmse[-1] - train_rmse[-1])
if gap > 0.1:
    diagnosis = "High Variance (Overfitting) - large gap between train & val error"
elif val_rmse[-1] > 0.8:
    diagnosis = "High Bias (Underfitting) - both errors are high"
else:
    diagnosis = "Good Fit - train & val errors are close and low"

print(f"Final Training RMSE  : {train_rmse[-1]:.4f}")
print(f"Final Validation RMSE: {val_rmse[-1]:.4f}")
print(f"Gap (overfit signal) : {gap:.4f}")
print(f"Diagnosis: {diagnosis}")

Final Training RMSE  : 0.9799
Final Validation RMSE: 1.0524
Gap (overfit signal) : 0.0725
Diagnosis: High Bias (Underfitting) - both errors are high
In [20]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Descale predictions back to original Weekly Sales ($)
y_pred_scaled = model.predict(X_scaled)

y_actual_original = scaler_y.inverse_transform(y_scaled.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()
y_pred_original   = scaler_y.inverse_transform(y_pred_scaled.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()

# Calculate RMSE in original dollar scale
residuals_original = y_actual_original - y_pred_original
mse_original       = np.mean(residuals_original ** 2)
rmse_original      = np.sqrt(mse_original)
mae_original       = np.mean(np.abs(residuals_original))
mean_sales         = y_actual_original.mean()
rmse_pct           = (rmse_original / mean_sales) * 100

print("=" * 50)
print("  Error in Original Scale (Weekly Sales $)")
print("=" * 50)
print(f"  Mean Weekly Sales      : ${mean_sales:>12,.0f}")
print(f"  RMSE                   : ${rmse_original:>12,.0f}")
print(f"  MAE                    : ${mae_original:>12,.0f}")
print(f"  RMSE as % of Mean Sales: {rmse_pct:>11.1f}%")
print("=" * 50)

# Plot: Actual vs Predicted + Residuals (original scale)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Left: Actual vs Predicted scatter
axes[0].scatter(y_actual_original, y_pred_original, alpha=0.3, color='steelblue', s=10)
min_val = min(y_actual_original.min(), y_pred_original.min())
max_val = max(y_actual_original.max(), y_pred_original.max())
axes[0].plot([min_val, max_val], [min_val, max_val], 'r--', linewidth=2, label='Perfect Prediction')
axes[0].set_title('Actual vs Predicted Weekly Sales', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Actual Weekly Sales ($)', fontsize=11)
axes[0].set_ylabel('Predicted Weekly Sales ($)', fontsize=11)
axes[0].legend(fontsize=10)
axes[0].grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

# Right: Residuals histogram (original scale)
axes[1].hist(residuals_original, bins=50, color='salmon', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.7)
axes[1].axvline(0, color='black', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label='Zero Error')
axes[1].axvline(rmse_original, color="crimson", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, label=f"+RMSE")
axes[1].axvline(-rmse_original, color="crimson", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, label=f"-RMSE")
axes[1].set_title('Residuals Distribution (Original Scale)', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Prediction Error ($)', fontsize=11)
axes[1].set_ylabel('Count', fontsize=11)
axes[1].legend(fontsize=9)
axes[1].grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

==================================================
  Error in Original Scale (Weekly Sales $)
==================================================
  Mean Weekly Sales      : $   1,046,965
  RMSE                   : $     557,097
  MAE                    : $     467,489
  RMSE as % of Mean Sales:        53.2%
==================================================
In [21]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# 1. Sort the indices based on actual sales values from lowest to highest
sorted_indices = np.argsort(y_actual_original)
actual_sorted = y_actual_original[sorted_indices]
pred_sorted = y_pred_original[sorted_indices]

# 2. Create the plot using subplots (avoiding plt.figure)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

# 3. Plot Actual vs Predicted lines
ax.plot(actual_sorted, color='black', linewidth=2, label='Actual Weekly Sales (Sorted)')
ax.plot(pred_sorted, color='crimson', alpha=0.6, linewidth=1.5, label='Predicted Weekly Sales')

# 4. Fill the area between them to highlight the errors (residuals)
ax.fill_between(range(len(actual_sorted)), actual_sorted, pred_sorted, 
                where=(actual_sorted > pred_sorted), color='blue', alpha=0.15, label='Underpredicting')
ax.fill_between(range(len(actual_sorted)), actual_sorted, pred_sorted, 
                where=(actual_sorted < pred_sorted), color='orange', alpha=0.15, label='Overpredicting')

# 5. Format labels and titles
ax.set_title('1D Comparison: Actual vs. Predicted Sales (Sorted by Actual Value)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Data Points (Sorted from Lowest to Highest Sales)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Weekly Sales ($)', fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=11, loc='upper left')
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

# Format y-axis numbers to look like readable currency (e.g., $1.5M)
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker
formatter = ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, pos: f'${x*1e-6:.1f}M')
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(formatter)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [22]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.feature_selection import mutual_info_regression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor

# 1. Load data
df_raw = pd.read_csv('data/Walmart_Sales.csv')
df_fi = df_raw.copy()

# Convert Date to numeric features
df_fi['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df_fi['Date'], dayfirst=True)
df_fi['Month'] = df_fi['Date'].dt.month
df_fi['Week']  = df_fi['Date'].dt.isocalendar().week.astype(int)
df_fi['Year']  = df_fi['Date'].dt.year
df_fi.drop(columns=['Date'], inplace=True)

X = df_fi.drop(columns=['Weekly_Sales'])
y = df_fi['Weekly_Sales']
features = X.columns.tolist()
print("Features:", features)

# 2. Pearson
pearson_corr = X.corrwith(y).abs().sort_values(ascending=False)

# 3. Spearman
spearman_corr = X.corrwith(y, method='spearman').abs().sort_values(ascending=False)

# 4. Mutual Information
mi_scores = mutual_info_regression(X, y, random_state=42)
mi_series = pd.Series(mi_scores, index=features).sort_values(ascending=False)

# 5. Random Forest
rf = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=150, random_state=42, n_jobs=-1)
rf.fit(X, y)
rf_importance = pd.Series(rf.feature_importances_, index=features).sort_values(ascending=False)

# 6. Plot
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(16, 11))
fig.suptitle('Feature Importance for Weekly Sales (Walmart Dataset)', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold', y=1.01)

def bar_plot(ax, series, title, xlabel, color_map):
    colors = plt.colormaps[color_map](np.linspace(0.85, 0.25, len(series)))
    bars = ax.barh(series.index[::-1], series.values[::-1], color=colors[::-1], edgecolor='white')
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_xlabel(xlabel, fontsize=11)
    ax.grid(axis='x', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
    for bar, val in zip(bars, series.values[::-1]):
        ax.text(bar.get_width() + 0.002, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
                f'{val:.3f}', va='center', fontsize=9)
    ax.spines[['top','right']].set_visible(False)

bar_plot(axes[0,0], pearson_corr,  '1. Pearson Correlation (Linear)',     '|Correlation|', 'Blues_r')
bar_plot(axes[0,1], spearman_corr, '2. Spearman Correlation (Monotonic)', '|Correlation|', 'Greens_r')
bar_plot(axes[1,0], mi_series,     '3. Mutual Information (Non-Linear)',  'MI Score',      'Oranges_r')
bar_plot(axes[1,1], rf_importance, '4. Random Forest Importance',         'Importance',    'Purples_r')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('feature_importance_walmart.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("Plot saved!")

# 7. Consensus Ranking
def normalize(s):
    return (s - s.min()) / (s.max() - s.min())

summary = pd.DataFrame({
    'Pearson'  : normalize(pearson_corr),
    'Spearman' : normalize(spearman_corr),
    'Mut_Info' : normalize(mi_series),
    'RF_Imp'   : normalize(rf_importance),
}).fillna(0)
summary['Consensus_Score'] = summary.mean(axis=1)
summary = summary.sort_values('Consensus_Score', ascending=False)
print('\n====  Consensus Feature Ranking (higher = more important)  ====')
print(summary.round(3).to_string())

Features: ['Store', 'Holiday_Flag', 'Temperature', 'Fuel_Price', 'CPI', 'Unemployment', 'Month', 'Week', 'Year']

Plot saved!

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optimizer_module
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# =====================================================================
# 1. DATA PREPROCESSING & CLEAN ONE-HOT ENCODING
# =====================================================================
# One-hot encode the Store column to map individual store baselines cleanly
df_encoded = pd.get_dummies(df_raw, columns=['Store'], drop_first=True)

# Isolate features and target
X_final = df_encoded.drop(columns=['Weekly_Sales', 'Date'], errors='ignore')
y_final = df_encoded['Weekly_Sales'].values.reshape(-1, 1)

# Scale features and target variables
scaler_X = StandardScaler()
scaler_y = StandardScaler()

X_final_scaled = scaler_X.fit_transform(X_final)
y_final_scaled = scaler_y.fit_transform(y_final)

# =====================================================================
# 2. MODEL ARCHITECTURE DEFINITION
# =====================================================================
class WalmartOneHotNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim):
        super(WalmartOneHotNet, self).__init__()
        self.hidden1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, 128)
        self.relu1 = nn.ReLU()
        self.dropout1 = nn.Dropout(0.1)
        
        self.hidden2 = nn.Linear(128, 64)
        self.relu2 = nn.ReLU()
        self.dropout2 = nn.Dropout(0.1)
        
        self.output_layer = nn.Linear(64, 1)
        
    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.dropout1(self.relu1(self.hidden1(x)))
        x = self.dropout2(self.relu2(self.hidden2(x)))
        x = self.output_layer(x)
        return x

# =====================================================================
# 3. CROSS-VALIDATION LEARNING CURVE CONFIGURATION
# =====================================================================
kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
subset_percentages = [0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0]

# Storage arrays to track the mean performance scores per data scale step
cv_train_scores = []
cv_val_scores = []

criterion = nn.MSELoss()
epochs = 100  # Reduced slightly per fold step to keep training snappy

print("⚡ Starting 5-Fold Cross-Validation Learning Curve Execution...")

for pct in subset_percentages:
    print(f"\n--- Slicing {pct*100:.0f}% of the Available Training Data ---")
    fold_train_losses = []
    fold_val_losses = []
    
    for fold, (train_idx, val_idx) in enumerate(kf.split(X_final_scaled)):
        # Calculate how many data samples match the current size subset step
        num_samples = int(len(train_idx) * pct)
        subset_train_idx = train_idx[:num_samples]
        
        # Create data subsets for this specific fold run
        X_tr, y_tr = X_final_scaled[subset_train_idx], y_final_scaled[subset_train_idx]
        X_va, y_va = X_final_scaled[val_idx], y_final_scaled[val_idx]
        
        # Convert slices cleanly to PyTorch Tensors
        X_tr_t = torch.tensor(X_tr, dtype=torch.float32)
        y_tr_t = torch.tensor(y_tr, dtype=torch.float32)
        X_va_t = torch.tensor(X_va, dtype=torch.float32)
        y_va_t = torch.tensor(y_va, dtype=torch.float32)
        
        # Initialize a fresh model instance to cleanly reset weights per fold
        model = WalmartOneHotNet(input_dim=X_final_scaled.shape[1])
        opt = optimizer_module.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.01, weight_decay=1e-4)
        
        # Create DataLoader loop for mini-batching optimization
        train_loader = DataLoader(TensorDataset(X_tr_t, y_tr_t), batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
        
        # Training loop execution
        for epoch in range(epochs):
            model.train()
            for batch_X, batch_y in train_loader:
                opt.zero_grad()
                loss = criterion(model(batch_X), batch_y)
                loss.backward()
                opt.step()
                
        # Evaluate performance on training slice and validation slice
        model.eval()
        with torch.no_grad():
            train_loss = criterion(model(X_tr_t), y_tr_t).item()
            val_loss = criterion(model(X_va_t), y_va_t).item()
            
        fold_train_losses.append(train_loss)
        fold_val_losses.append(val_loss)
        print(f"  Fold {fold+1}/5 completed. Val MSE: {val_loss:.4f}")
        
    # Average the metrics across all 5 folds for this subset size
    cv_train_scores.append(np.mean(fold_train_losses))
    cv_val_scores.append(np.mean(fold_val_losses))

# =====================================================================
# 4. PLOT CROSS-VALIDATION LEARNING CURVE
# =====================================================================
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.5))
sample_sizes = [int(len(X_final_scaled) * 0.8 * pct) for pct in subset_percentages]

ax.plot(sample_sizes, cv_train_scores, marker='o', color='steelblue', linewidth=2.5, label='Cross-Validation Training Error')
ax.plot(sample_sizes, cv_val_scores, marker='s', color='crimson', linewidth=2.5, label='Cross-Validation Generalization Error')

ax.set_title('Cross-Validation Learning Curve (MSE vs. Training Set Size)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Number of Training Samples Given to Model', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Mean Squared Error (Standardized Scale)', fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

⚡ Starting 5-Fold Cross-Validation Learning Curve Execution...

  Fold 2/5 completed. Val MSE: 1.0166
  Fold 3/5 completed. Val MSE: 1.4029
  Fold 4/5 completed. Val MSE: 1.1234
  Fold 5/5 completed. Val MSE: 1.4425

In [27]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

# 1. FIX: Use 'y_va' instead of 'y_val' (from the last CV fold in memory)
y_val_original = scaler_y.inverse_transform(y_va.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()

# 2. Sort the indices based on actual validation sales values (lowest to highest)
sorted_indices = np.argsort(y_val_original)
actual_sorted = y_val_original[sorted_indices]

# 3. Match the predictions to the validation split size and sort them using the same indices
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    # FIX: Use 'X_va_t' instead of 'X_val_t'
    y_val_pred_scaled = model(X_va_t).numpy()

# Inverse transform the predictions (they are already 2D from the model output)
y_val_pred_original = scaler_y.inverse_transform(y_val_pred_scaled).flatten()
pred_sorted = y_val_pred_original[sorted_indices]

# 4. Create the plot using subplots
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

# 5. Plot Actual vs Predicted lines
ax.plot(actual_sorted, color='black', linewidth=2, label='Actual Weekly Sales (Sorted Validation)')
ax.plot(pred_sorted, color='crimson', alpha=0.6, linewidth=1.5, label='Predicted Weekly Sales')

# 6. Fill the area between them to highlight the errors (residuals)
ax.fill_between(range(len(actual_sorted)), actual_sorted, pred_sorted, where=(actual_sorted > pred_sorted), color='blue', alpha=0.15, label='Underpredicting')
ax.fill_between(range(len(actual_sorted)), actual_sorted, pred_sorted, where=(actual_sorted < pred_sorted), color='orange', alpha=0.15, label='Overpredicting')

# 7. Format labels and titles
ax.set_title('1D Comparison: Actual vs. Predicted Sales (Validation Set - L2 Regularized)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Data Points (Sorted from Lowest to Highest Sales)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Weekly Sales ($)', fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=11, loc='upper left')
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

# Format y-axis numbers to look like readable currency (e.g., $1.5M)
formatter = ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, pos: f'${x*1e-6:.1f}M')
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(formatter)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [28]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scipy.stats as stats
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

# 1. Calculate the raw residuals (Actual - Predicted) in original dollar scale
# (Using the arrays generated in your previous cell)
residuals = y_val_original - y_val_pred_original

# 2. Compute the sample metrics for the theoretical normal curve
mu = np.mean(residuals)
sigma = np.std(residuals)

# 3. Create the plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

# Plot the actual histogram of residuals (density=True scales it to fit the probability curve)
count, bins, ignored = ax.hist(residuals, bins=50, density=True, alpha=0.6, 
                               color='steelblue', edgecolor='white', label='Model Residuals')

# Generate points along the x-axis to build a perfect bell curve matching our mu and sigma
xmin, xmax = ax.get_xlim()
x_axis = np.linspace(xmin, xmax, 100)
fitted_normal = stats.norm.pdf(x_axis, mu, sigma)

# Plot the theoretical normal distribution curve
ax.plot(x_axis, fitted_normal, color='crimson', linewidth=2.5, 
        linestyle='--', label=f'Ideal Normal ($\mu$={mu*1e-3:.1f}k, $\sigma$={sigma*1e-6:.2f}M)')

# Add a vertical guide line at exactly zero error
ax.axvline(0, color='black', linestyle=':', alpha=0.7, label='Zero Error Reference')

# 4. Format labels and styling
ax.set_title('Residuals Distribution vs. Theoretical Normal Curve', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Prediction Error / Residual ($)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Probability Density', fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=11, loc='upper right')
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)

# Format x-axis numbers into clean currency markers (e.g., -$0.5M, $0.5M)
formatter = ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, pos: f'${x * 1e-6:.1f}M' if x >= 0 else f'-${abs(x) * 1e-6:.1f}M')
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(formatter)

plt.tight_layout()
# Save the figure to your current working directory
plt.savefig('residual_distribution.png', dpi=300)

from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, mean_absolute_error
import pandas as pd

# 1. Calculate the evaluation metrics using our descaled validation arrays
r2 = r2_score(y_val_original, y_val_pred_original)
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_val_original, y_val_pred_original)

# 2. Print a beautifully formatted summary report
print("=" * 45)
print("     WALMART NEURAL NETWORK FINAL PERFORMANCE     ")
print("=" * 45)
print(f"R² Score (Variance Explained): {r2:.4f} ({r2 * 100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Mean Absolute Error (MAE):     ${mae:,.2f}")
print("=" * 45)

# 3. Quick benchmark check
if r2 > 0.90:
    print("🚀 Status: Elite Tier Performance! The model has mastered store scales.")
elif r2 > 0.75:
    print("👍 Status: Strong Performance, but room to grow on holiday peaks.")
else:
    print("⚠️ Status: Underfitting. The model needs more features or capacity.")

In [34]:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, mean_absolute_error
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

# 1. Configuration
n_bootstraps = 100 
epochs = 50       
bootstrapped_r2 = []
bootstrapped_mae = []

# FIX: Changed X_train_t to X_tr_t
n_train_samples = len(X_tr_t)

print("=" * 60)
print(f"🚀 STARTING {n_bootstraps} BOOTSTRAPPED TRAINING LOOPS")
print("=" * 60)

for b in range(n_bootstraps):
    # FIX: Changed X_train_t to X_tr_t
    boot_indices = np.random.choice(n_train_samples, size=n_train_samples, replace=True)
    X_train_boot = X_tr_t[boot_indices]
    y_train_boot = y_tr_t[boot_indices]
    
    # 3. Initialize a FRESH, untrained model 
    # ⚠️ REMEMBER: Replace YourModelClass() with your actual model class name and arguments!
    boot_model = WalmartOneHotNet(input_dim=X_final_scaled.shape[1])
    
    # Re-initialize your optimizer and loss function
    optimizer = optim.Adam(boot_model.parameters(), lr=0.001) 
    criterion = nn.MSELoss() 
    
    # 4. Train the Bootstrapped Model
    boot_model.train()
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        outputs = boot_model(X_train_boot)
        loss = criterion(outputs, y_train_boot)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        
    # 5. Evaluate on the FIXED Validation Set
    boot_model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        val_preds_scaled = boot_model(X_va_t).numpy()
        
    # Inverse transform to original scale
    val_preds_original = scaler_y.inverse_transform(val_preds_scaled).flatten()
    
    # Calculate metrics for this specific bootstrap run
    r2 = r2_score(y_val_original, val_preds_original)
    mae = mean_absolute_error(y_val_original, val_preds_original)
    
    bootstrapped_r2.append(r2)
    bootstrapped_mae.append(mae)
    
    # Print progress every 5 loops
    if (b + 1) % 5 == 0 or (b + 1) == 1:
        print(f"Bootstrap {b+1}/{n_bootstraps} completed -> R²: {r2:.4f} | MAE: ${mae:,.0f}")

# 6. Calculate 95% Confidence Intervals
r2_ci_lower, r2_ci_upper = np.percentile(bootstrapped_r2, [2.5, 97.5])
mae_ci_lower, mae_ci_upper = np.percentile(bootstrapped_mae, [2.5, 97.5])

# 7. Print the Summary
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("     📈 TRUE MODEL PERFORMANCE ESTIMATES (95% CI)     ")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"R² Score Mean: {np.mean(bootstrapped_r2):.4f}")
print(f"R² 95% CI:     [{r2_ci_lower:.4f}, {r2_ci_upper:.4f}]\n")
print(f"MAE Mean:      ${np.mean(bootstrapped_mae):,.2f}")
print(f"MAE 95% CI:    [${mae_ci_lower:,.2f}, ${mae_ci_upper:,.2f}]")
print("=" * 60)

============================================================
🚀 STARTING 100 BOOTSTRAPPED TRAINING LOOPS
============================================================

Bootstrap 5/100 completed -> R²: 0.9120 | MAE: $101,577

Notes¶

Experiments¶
